title: Authorization Service (RBAC / ABAC) — System Design
description: Design a centralized authorization service that answers 'can subject S perform action A on object O?' for every request across many applications. The themes are the policy model (roles vs attributes vs relationships), the low-latency check hot path, consistency of permission changes, and listing what a user can access.

nodes.label: Application Service
nodes.description: Calls authz on every protected action: 'can user U do A on object O?'

nodes.label: Authz Client SDK
nodes.description: In-app library that calls the authz service and caches recent decisions.

nodes.label: Check API
nodes.description: Stateless front door evaluating permission checks at low latency.

nodes.label: Policy Engine
nodes.description: Evaluates roles/attributes/relations against policy to a decision.

nodes.label: Policy / Schema Store
nodes.description: Versioned namespace schemas, relation rewrites, RBAC roles, and ABAC policies plus their rollout state (draft/shadow/active).

nodes.label: Decision Cache
nodes.description: Caches recent check results, keyed by (subject, relation, object) + version.

nodes.label: Expanded-Set Index
nodes.description: Precomputed reverse index: which objects a subject can access (for list/filter).

nodes.label: Change Log / Versioning
nodes.description: Ordered log of tuple/policy changes; provides consistency tokens.

nodes.label: Materializer
nodes.description: Consumes changes to update expanded-set indexes and invalidate caches.

nodes.label: Admin / Policy Author
nodes.description: Defines roles, policies, and grants relationships.

requirementsDiagram: Check[can S do A on O?]

functional: Answer a permission check: allow/deny for (subject, action, object).
Support roles (RBAC) and attribute/relationship-based rules (ABAC / ReBAC).
Grant and revoke permissions and relationships.
List the objects a subject can access (and the subjects who can access an object).
Centralized policy used by many applications.
Author, test, roll out, and roll back policy/schema changes; explain and audit decisions.
Isolate tenants across tuples, policies, caches, and list indexes.
nonFunctional: Very low latency: checks are on every request's critical path (sub-10ms p99).
Correctness: never allow what should be denied; deny-by-default.
Bounded staleness with a knob: a revoke must be honored quickly, and callers can demand a consistent read.
Highly available — an authz outage shouldn't take down every app.

capacity.label: Checks per second
capacity.note: Every protected action; the dominant load.

capacity.label: Consistency
capacity.note: Default cached/fast; opt-in consistent for sensitive ops.

capacity.label: Cache hit rate / slow path
capacity.note: At 99% SDK+decision-cache hits, only ~1% (≈10k/s of 1M) reaches the policy engine; design the slow path for that, not for 1M/s.

capacity.label: Graph depth / fan-out
capacity.note: Bound the reachability walk (depth + per-node fan-out budget) so one check is a few tuple-page reads, not an unbounded traversal; cycle-detect.

capacity.label: Latency budget (p99)
capacity.note: Hot path is an in-process SDK hit (~0); on a miss the ≈10ms budget splits across network, decision-cache probe, and a bounded engine eval over tuple-store pages.

capacity.label: Tuple-store partitioning
capacity.note: Billions of tuples shard by object for forward checks; a secondary subject-keyed access pattern (and the reverse index) serves membership/listing.

capacity.label: Change log / audit
capacity.note: Partition the change log for materializer parallelism on group fan-out; if every decision is audited, ~1M records/s drives a tiered (hot→cold) retention budget.

api.description: Evaluate a single permission check. consistency selects the serving path: minimize_latency (any fresh-enough cache) or min_token (at least as fresh as a prior write). For the full matched-path trace use the privileged /v1/check:explain endpoint instead.

Idempotent, conditionally-applied grant/revoke of relationship tuples. idempotencyKey dedupes retries; precondition makes it a compare-and-set so concurrent edits don't clobber each other; actor/reason/requestId become the audit record.

List objects a subject can access for a relation, scoped by type/tenant and paginated (the result set may be millions). Served from the expanded-set index; min_token requires the index to be at least that fresh.

List the subjects who can access an object for a relation (the symmetric query to list-objects), served from the subject-set reverse index. By default returns direct grants plus set references (e.g. group:eng#member) rather than materializing every effective user; pass expand:true to expand a set ref into bounded, paginated user pages. min_token requires the shard to be at least that fresh.

Evaluate a bounded candidate set in one round trip — used to filter the objects on a page or a search result set, instead of N single checks or a full list-objects read. The candidate set is capped; consistency applies to the whole batch.

Debug a surprising decision: returns the decision plus the matched relation path, the policy/tuple versions used, the reason code, and which ABAC caveats were evaluated. Privileged and rate-limited — it reveals relationship structure, so subject/object identifiers in the trace are redacted per the caller's scope.
api.response: { "allowed": true, "reasonCode": "path_found", "policyVersion": 14, "matchedPath": [ "doc:9#editor <- folder:3#editor (rewrite)", "folder:3#editor <- group:eng#member", "group:eng#member <- user:42" ], "caveatsEvaluated": [ { "name": "business_hours", "result": true } ], "tupleVersions": [1207, 1190], "redactions": ["sibling subjects omitted"] }

api.description: Publish a namespace/relation-rewrite schema or policy version. Versions are staged (draft → shadow → active) and can be rolled back; a write returns the new version and its rollout status so a bad schema is reversible.

dataModel.name: relation_tuples
dataModel.note: Authoritative relationships (Zanzibar-style). The core of ReBAC. Partitioned by (tenant, object).

fields.name: tenant
fields.type: string (isolation boundary; part of every key)

fields.name: subject
fields.type: string (user:42 or set ref like group:eng#member)

fields.name: caveat
fields.type: optional predicate (ABAC condition on subject/object/env attrs)

dataModel.name: policy_versions
dataModel.note: Namespace/relation-rewrite schemas and ABAC policies, versioned for safe rollout and rollback.

fields.name: rewrites
fields.type: list (relation rewrite rules; e.g. editor implies viewer)

fields.name: validation
fields.type: record (relations resolve, recursion/depth budget ok, no unhandled cycles, migration-compatible) — gates draft→shadow

fields.name: shadow_metrics
fields.type: record (allow/deny delta vs active version, sampled diffs) accumulated during shadow before promotion

fields.name: activated_at_token / rolled_back_from
fields.type: token / bigint (change-log point of activation; decisions cached under a rolled-back version are invalidated by version, like a tuple change)

dataModel.name: expanded_set_chunks
dataModel.note: Derived reverse index, chunked for scale: a subject's accessible objects per (relation, objectType) split into paginated shards (not one giant set).

fields.name: object_ids
fields.type: ordered list<string> (one bounded shard; pageToken walks shards)

dataModel.name: subject_set_chunks
dataModel.note: Derived object-to-subject reverse index for 'who can access this object', chunked per (relation, subjectType). Stores direct grants and set refs (group:eng#member), not always every effective user — a doc shared with a 1M-member group is a few set refs, not a million rows. Callers can expand a set ref on demand (bounded, paginated).

fields.name: subjects
fields.type: ordered list<string> (direct user ids + set refs; pageToken walks shards)

fields.name: kind
fields.type: enum (direct | set_ref; set_ref entries are expanded only if requested)

fields.name: applied_version
fields.type: bigint (change-log watermark this chunk reflects; gates min_token reads)

dataModel.name: relationship_changes
dataModel.note: The ordered change log as records: drives materialization and doubles as the grant/revoke audit trail.

fields.name: version
fields.type: bigint (log position; basis for consistency tokens)

dataModel.name: decision_audit_log
dataModel.note: Per-decision record for compliance, support, and incident review. At 1M checks/s, logging every record is cost-prohibitive: ingest is async (the check never blocks on it; on backpressure it sheds sampled allows, never the decision itself), denies/admin/sensitive actions are logged in full while routine allows are sampled or aggregated, and records age from a hot searchable store to cold object storage on a retention/deletion schedule. Subject/object ids and reason/explain traces are subject to privacy redaction.

tradeoff: An explicit edge gives every caller the same TLS, tenant routing, request size, coarse quota, and WAF controls before the low-latency Check API is reached.
The edge should shape traffic and pass authenticated context, not own the final allow/deny logic; otherwise policy drifts back into infrastructure config.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed API edge with WAF, quota, access logs, and health checks removes most custom reverse-proxy and coarse rate-limit plumbing.

tradeoff: A Zanzibar-style ReBAC engine fits object sharing, groups, folders, and ownership because the core data is relationship tuples and graph reachability.
OPA/Cedar-style policy evaluation is strong for ABAC and policy-as-code, but it still needs a relationship store and listing pipeline for fine-grained object sharing.
Provider-managed policy services reduce policy hosting and rollout work, but they may not replace a generic, multi-tenant ReBAC graph for every product object.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed fine-grained policy service can remove the custom parser, evaluator sandbox, test bench, and policy distribution plane when its policy model matches the product.

technologyChoices.concern: Check API runtime and SDK deployment
tradeoff: A dedicated stateless check tier keeps permission checks horizontally scalable and isolates them from application release cadence.
A sidecar or in-process SDK gives near-zero-latency hot hits, but every application must ship the same cache-key, token, and fail-closed behavior.
Serverless runtimes are attractive for spiky admin and explain paths; the sub-10ms check hot path usually wants warm, pooled compute.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed container or serverless runtime with autoscaling, health checks, and rollout controls removes most custom fleet management around the Check API.

tradeoff: A wide-column or KV store fits billions of tuple pages partitioned by tenant and object, but secondary subject lookups and conditional writes need careful modeling.
Relational or distributed SQL stores make tuple uniqueness, preconditions, and policy-version transactions straightforward, but hot objects and fan-out require sharding discipline.
A graph database is not automatically the best fit: checks need bounded, explainable reachability over versioned tuples, not arbitrary graph exploration.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A store with conditional writes, change streams, TTLs, and multi-region replicas removes much of the custom compare-and-set, dedupe, and replication scaffolding around tuple writes.

tradeoff: Policy/schema writes are low volume but correctness-heavy: draft, shadow, active, and rolled-back versions must be atomic and auditable.
A database keeps policy history and activation tokens near the tuple store; a config service is better for safe rollout flags and fast distribution.
Separating policy validation from activation prevents a bad schema from invalidating live checks.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed config store with feature flags and revision history can remove the bespoke rollout switchboard, but not the domain-specific policy validator.

tradeoff: The in-process SDK cache absorbs the hottest repeated checks; a shared decision cache smooths misses across application instances.
Cache keys must include tenant, subject, relation/action, object, policy version, and decision version; a stale or partial key is a security bug.
Fail-open improves availability but can create wrong allows; security-sensitive paths should fail closed or require a fresh token.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed cache with clustering, failover, metrics, and eviction controls removes most cache-node operations, but not the invalidation and freshness semantics.

tradeoff: A retained ordered log is the natural source of consistency tokens, materializer replay, cache invalidation, and grant/revoke audit.
A queue is simpler for per-message retries and DLQs, but weaker for replaying the full authorization history at a target version.
Global ordering is expensive; partition by tenant or object namespace and make the token semantics explicit.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed stream with retention, consumer lag metrics, partition scaling, and DLQ integration removes custom offset storage and replay plumbing.

tradeoff: A wide-column or KV index fits paginated subject-to-objects and object-to-subject shards without creating one giant expanded set.
Search indexes help when authorization filters must combine with content search, but index freshness and delete semantics become security-critical.
Keeping direct grants and set references instead of fully expanding large groups avoids write amplification at the cost of bounded on-demand expansion.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed scalable KV or search index removes much of the shard rebalancing, capacity planning, and backfill storage work for list-objects and list-subjects.

tradeoff: Streaming workers keep reverse indexes and cache invalidations close to the change log, which protects revoke latency.
Batch repair remains necessary because derived indexes drift after bugs, partial outages, or schema migrations.
The materializer must be idempotent and version-aware; exactly-once infrastructure does not remove the need for applied_version checks.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed stream processors and batch runners remove much of the custom worker lease, retry, backoff, and backfill scheduling code.

technologyChoices.concern: Decision audit log and explain trace analytics
tradeoff: The check path should never block on audit ingestion; backpressure should shed sampled allow logs before it affects decisions.
Deny, admin, policy-change, and explain events deserve full-fidelity retention, while routine allow decisions can be sampled or aggregated.
Explain traces are useful for support and incident review but expose relationship structure, so they need redaction and privileged access.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: A managed logging, stream, and search stack removes most collector, hot-index, cold-retention, and dashboard machinery for decision records.

tradeoff: Regional check replicas reduce latency and keep applications alive during an authz-region outage.
Single-writer tuple changes simplify token ordering, but remote revokes wait for replication; active-active writes improve locality but complicate conflict handling.
The security question is explicit: during partition or failover, does a caller prefer deny, stale allow within a bound, or wait for a fresh token?
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed global databases and traffic managers remove much of the replication and failover control plane, but not the product decision about stale reads and wrong allows.

tradeoff: Track p99 check latency, cache hit rate, stale-cache rejections, min_token wait time, graph depth, tuple-store read fan-out, materializer lag, and index applied_version lag.
Correctness SLOs need explicit signals for wrong-allow incidents, deny-by-default fallbacks, policy rollback rate, and shadow/active policy diffs.
Security audit retention and operational metrics overlap, but they should not be the same store or access-control boundary.
technologyChoices.makesIrrelevant: Managed observability removes most collector, dashboard, alert, and trace-storage work, but the authorization-specific semantic metrics still must be designed.

patterns.name: Centralized authorization service
patterns.what: One service answers all permission checks; apps don't embed policy logic.
patterns.whenToUse: Many services needing consistent, auditable access decisions.

patterns.name: RBAC
patterns.what: Grant permissions to roles, assign roles to subjects.

patterns.name: ReBAC (relationship tuples)
patterns.what: Model permissions as a graph of (object, relation, subject) tuples; check = graph reachability.

patterns.name: Decision caching with versioning
patterns.what: Cache check results keyed by a consistency token so revokes invalidate correctly.
patterns.whenToUse: Sub-10ms checks under heavy read load.

patterns.name: Consistency tokens (zookies)
patterns.what: A token from a write lets a caller demand a check at-least-as-fresh-as that write.
patterns.whenToUse: When a recent grant/revoke must be reflected (no 'new enemy' bug).

patterns.name: Materialized reverse index
patterns.what: Precompute subject->objects so 'list what I can see' is a lookup, not a graph walk per object.
patterns.whenToUse: List/filter endpoints over large object sets.

steps.title: 1. Naive: Hardcode Permission Checks in Each Service
description: Start with the simplest thing that works. Each application embeds its own permission logic inline — `if user.role == 'admin'` (or a handful of such checks) scattered through the code, reading whatever role/attribute data it has handy. There is no central authority; every service decides for itself.

This is the path of least resistance — and it exposes the problems the rest of the design solves. First, every team reimplements checks slightly differently, so the same question gets different answers across services (inconsistent and bug-prone); each service also reinvents the tenant boundary, so a cross-tenant leak in one service isn't caught anywhere central (tenant isolation becomes a first-class requirement later). Second, there's no central place to see or change who-can-do-what: a policy change means editing and redeploying many services, and — with each decision made inline and unlogged — there's no audit trail of who was allowed to do what and why. (Auditability becomes a recurring design goal from here on, not an afterthought.) Third, hardcoded roles can't express real product sharing like 'people who can edit THIS specific document'.
So the baseline motivates everything that follows: centralize authorization behind one model and one check API, so policy is defined, evaluated, and audited in a single place.

view.caption: The application service reads roles from the policy/schema store and hardcodes its own check, while an admin defines roles and policies directly in that store.

steps.decisionPrompt: If every service hardcodes its own `if role == admin` checks, how do you change a policy everywhere at once, audit decisions, or express 'editors of THIS document'?
whyNow: Establishing the naive baseline first makes it obvious why authorization must be centralized into one model and one check API — consistency, auditability, central change, and expressiveness are exactly what scattered inline checks can't give you.

recap.after: Each service hardcodes its own role checks against ad hoc data.
recap.newRisk: Inconsistent across services, no central change or audit, and can't express fine-grained per-object permissions.

traps.trap: Embedding authorization logic in every service.
traps.why: Inconsistent, unauditable, impossible to change centrally, and can't express per-object/relationship permissions.
traps.instead: A centralized check API with one policy model (relationship tuples + roles/attributes).

steps.title: 2. Choose the Authorization Model
description: Centralize authz so apps just ask 'can S do A on O?' and never embed policy. The first decision is the model. RBAC (roles → permissions) is simple but coarse — it can't express 'editors of THIS document'. ABAC adds attribute rules (department, time, resource tags). ReBAC models permissions as a relationship graph of (object, relation, subject) tuples.

ReBAC (the Zanzibar approach) is the most expressive for real product sharing: a check becomes graph reachability — is there a path from the subject to the object via relations (ownership, group membership, folder inheritance)?

ReBAC needs more than raw tuples: each object type has a namespace schema defining its valid relations and relation rewrites — `document#viewer includes group#member`, `folder#editor implies document#editor` for contained docs. Rewrites are what make group/folder inheritance composable, and they're where you set depth/cycle limits so reachability stays bounded.

A schema is not just evaluation logic; it's a versioned contract many applications depend on. Changing a rewrite is a migration: it can silently widen or narrow access across every app using that namespace, so schema changes are validated and rolled out (shadow → active) rather than edited live (covered in Step 7).
Store relation tuples as the source of truth; layer RBAC roles and ABAC predicates (as caveats on tuples) on top where they fit. Deny by default.

view.caption: The application calls the authz client SDK, which hits the check API; the API has the policy engine evaluate by reading both the relationship/tuple store and the policy/schema store.

options.name: Relationship-based (ReBAC, Zanzibar-style) (default)
options.description: Permissions are stored as (object, relation, subject) tuples in a relation store, and a check is graph reachability over ownership, group, and folder relations.
pros: Expresses 'editors of THIS document' directly as (object, relation, subject) tuples, which roles cannot.
Group and folder membership compose: editor-of-folder implies editor-of-its-docs via relation rewrites.
One uniform tuple model serves every app, so the Policy Engine evaluates the same way everywhere.
cons: A check may walk a deep group/folder graph, so the Check API needs caching and an expanded-set index to stay fast.
Modeling everything as tuples is unfamiliar and easy to get subtly wrong.
Listing accessible objects requires a precomputed reverse index, adding pipeline machinery.

view.caption: The policy engine answers a check by walking the relationship/tuple store graph, with the policy/schema store available, as the SDK and check API forward the request.

options.name: Role-based (RBAC) only
options.description: Users are assigned roles and roles carry permissions, so a check looks up the subject's roles against the action's required role in the role store.
pros: Simple and well understood: assign users to roles, roles to permissions.
Cheap checks - look up the user's roles and the action's required role, no graph walk.
Easy to audit: a user's roles fully describe what they can do.
cons: Cannot express per-object permissions like 'editor of THIS document' without a role explosion.
Cross-cutting rules (owner, shared-with) don't fit the role model cleanly.
Teams end up bolting ad-hoc resource checks onto RBAC, recreating the naive hardcoded problem.

view.caption: The policy engine answers a check by looking up the subject's roles against the action's required role in the policy/schema store, reached via the SDK and check API.

options.name: Attribute-based (ABAC) policies
options.description: The policy engine evaluates boolean rules over subject, object, and context attributes (department, time, region) fetched at check time, rather than stored relationships.
pros: Policies are boolean rules over subject/object/context attributes (department == owner_dept, time-of-day, region).
Very flexible for compliance and context-aware rules without enumerating relationships.
Decouples policy from data: change a rule centrally without re-granting tuples.
cons: Answering 'who can access THIS object' or 'which objects can this user see' requires evaluating rules over data, which is slow to list.
Rule sets become hard to reason about and test as conditions accumulate.
Needs fresh attribute data at check time, adding lookups to the hot path.

view.caption: The policy engine evaluates attribute rules, reading the policy/schema store for policy plus the relationship/tuple store for any relations behind the SDK and check API.

steps.decisionPrompt: RBAC, ABAC, or ReBAC? How do you express 'people who can edit THIS specific document'?

concepts.definition: Roles->perms; attribute predicates; or a relationship graph where check = reachability.
concepts.whyItMatters: The model determines expressiveness, performance, and how grants are reasoned about.
concepts.example: ReBAC tuple: (doc:9, editor, user:42); group membership and folder inheritance chain via tuples.

concepts.definition: Per-object-type definitions of valid relations and how they derive (inheritance/implication) from others.
concepts.whyItMatters: Rewrites make group/folder inheritance composable and are where depth/cycle limits live, keeping reachability bounded.
concepts.example: document#viewer includes group#member; folder#editor implies document#editor for contained docs.

whyNow: The model choice frames everything; leading with ReBAC tuples + deny-by-default is the modern, expressive answer interviewers look for.

recap.after: One model (relationship tuples + roles/attrs), centrally evaluated.
recap.newRisk: Evaluating a graph walk per request is too slow on the hot path.

steps.title: 3. The Low-Latency Check Path
description: A check is on every request, so it must be single-digit-ms. A check evaluates the policy: for ReBAC, walk the relationship graph from object to subject (is user:42 an editor of doc:9? → doc:9 sits in folder:3, which user:42 owns), short-circuiting on the first allow.

Bound the walk: cap depth and per-node fan-out, detect cycles, and cache intermediate set memberships, not just final decisions. Cache key shape is `(tenant, subject, relation, object, policyVersion)` with the entry's applied change-log version stored for freshness; cache denies too (negative caching) so repeated denied probes stay cheap. Besides final verdicts, cache the expensive sub-results — group membership, resolved relation rewrites, and compiled policies.

Most checks are repeats (same user, same object), so the decision cache absorbs the bulk of load. Keep the check API stateless and horizontally scaled; ship an SDK that caches recent decisions in-process to cut even the network hop for hot checks.

Watch for hot keys: a viral document, a huge shared group, or one busy tenant can concentrate traffic on a single cache shard or tuple partition even when total QPS looks fine. Protect the hot path with per-key request coalescing (single-flight a cold key so a stampede becomes one evaluation), replicating/sharding hot entries, and per-tenant quotas so one tenant can't starve the rest.

view.caption: The check API consults the decision cache for a prior verdict and only falls through to the policy engine, which walks the relationship/tuple store, on a cache miss.

options.name: Decision cache in front of the engine (default)
options.description: A decision cache keyed by a consistency version sits ahead of the policy engine, returning prior (subject, relation, object) verdicts on a hit and walking the graph only on a miss.
pros: Cache hits return (subject, relation, object) decisions in well under 10ms, skipping the graph walk entirely.
Each entry carries the change-log version it was computed at, so a check can tell whether the entry is fresh enough for its min_token.
Absorbs hot, repeated checks (the same user opening the same docs) cheaply.
cons: A cold cache or low hit rate still pays the full PolicyEngine + RelStore walk.
Cache invalidation on tuple changes is the hard part - get it wrong and you serve a revoked grant.
Adds a cache tier to operate, size, and keep warm across regions.

view.caption: The check API checks the decision cache first and only on a miss evaluates via the policy engine over the relationship/tuple store.

options.name: Precompute the expanded set (materialize reachability)
options.description: An indexer consumes the change log and materializes each subject's reachable objects into an expanded-set index, turning a check into a single index lookup.
pros: The deep group/folder walk is done once offline, so a check is a single index lookup - predictable single-digit ms.
The same expanded-set index also powers 'list objects this user can see' for free.
No per-check graph traversal, so tail latency is flat under load.
cons: Materialization lags writes: a fresh grant isn't checkable until the Materializer catches up.
Expanded sets can be huge for users in many large groups, costing storage.
More moving parts (ChangeLog + Materializer + index) to build and keep correct.

view.caption: The check API serves a list from the precomputed expanded-set index, which a materializer keeps current by consuming changes from the change log.

options.name: Walk the graph live on every check
options.description: Every check traverses the relation store graph from object to subject in real time, with no cache or precomputed index between the API and the authoritative tuples.
pros: Always reflects the current tuples - no caching or materialization lag to reason about.
Simplest possible data plane: one authoritative RelStore, no derived state.
No invalidation logic, so revokes are honored immediately by construction.
cons: A deep hierarchy means many RelStore round-trips per check, blowing the 10ms budget.
Hot objects hammer the RelStore, making it the scaling bottleneck.
Tail latency is unbounded as group/folder depth grows.

view.caption: Every check goes through the check API to the policy engine, which walks the relationship/tuple store graph live with no cache or precomputed index in between.

steps.decisionPrompt: A check requires walking a deep group/folder hierarchy. How do you keep it under 10ms?

concepts.definition: Evaluate reachability, caching set memberships and decisions to bound work.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns a potentially deep graph traversal into a mostly-cached fast lookup.
concepts.example: Cache 'user:42 in group:eng' and the final 'user:42 can edit doc:9'.

whyNow: The check is the hot path; framing it as a cached, bounded graph walk is the core performance argument.

recap.after: Cached, short-circuiting checks meeting the latency budget.
recap.newRisk: Caching makes revokes dangerous — a stale allow could leak access.

flows.name: Check: SDK cache → decision cache → graph walk
flows.note: A check tries the in-process SDK cache, then the decision cache; only a miss evaluates the (bounded, short-circuiting) graph walk in the policy engine.

steps.title: 4. Consistency: Honor Revokes Safely
description: Caching introduces the central authz hazard: after a revoke, a stale cached 'allow' could leak access (Zanzibar calls the inverse the 'new enemy' problem). You need a consistency knob.

Each write to the relationship store goes through an ordered change log and returns a consistency token (a 'zookie'). A caller can pass that token to a later check, meaning 'evaluate at least as fresh as this change' — so right after a revoke, the app demands a consistent check.

Make the guarantee precise via watermarks: every cache entry, index shard, and region carries the change-log version it has applied. A default check may use any cached decision within a bounded staleness window. A `min_token` check may use the cache only if that entry's (and the serving region's) applied version is ≥ the token; otherwise the check does NOT serve the stale entry — it re-evaluates fresh against the policy engine / tuple store, waits briefly for the watermark to catch up, routes to a fresher region, or returns a retryable consistency error. It never serves a stale allow. Async invalidation (next step) is an optimization that raises the hit rate; the watermark check is the actual safety property.

Be explicit about what happens when the fresh path can't complete within the latency budget. The fallback is a fixed order: (1) serve from cache if its watermark already ≥ token; (2) else re-evaluate fresh against the engine/tuple store; (3) if that won't finish in budget, wait briefly for the watermark, then route to a fresher region; (4) if still unmet, return a retryable consistency error — never a stale allow, and for sensitive actions deny/step-up rather than retry.
So default checks are fast and slightly stale; sensitive operations opt into a token. This makes the staleness-vs-latency tradeoff explicit and per-call.

view.caption: An admin's grant/revoke appends to the change log via the relationship/tuple store; the materializer advances the cache's applied-version watermark, and the check API compares its cached decision against the caller's min_token.

options.name: Consistency tokens from the change log (default)
options.description: Each write returns a change-log version token, and a check passing that token is evaluated at least as fresh as the change, never reading a pre-revoke cached decision.
pros: After a revoke, the caller passes a token >= the change's version, so the check can't read a pre-revoke cached decision.
Gives precise read-after-write for security-critical revokes without making every check fully synchronous.
The ChangeLog ordering is the single source of truth for 'has this change taken effect yet'.
cons: Callers (or the SDK) must thread the consistency token through requests.
A check at a high token may have to wait for the Materializer/cache to reach that version, adding latency.
More protocol surface than a plain cache lookup.

view.caption: An admin's grant/revoke writes the relationship/tuple store, which appends to the change log whose version token gates whether the check API trusts the decision cache.

options.name: Short cache TTL (bound the staleness window)
options.description: Cached decisions simply expire after a fixed interval, so a revoke is guaranteed honored once the entry's TTL elapses, with no tokens threaded through requests.
pros: Dead simple: cached decisions expire after, say, 10s, so a revoke is honored within that window.
No tokens to thread; the Check API stays a plain cache-then-engine lookup.
Predictable worst-case staleness that you can tune per sensitivity.
cons: There is always a window where a revoked user is still allowed - unacceptable for high-stakes actions.
Short TTLs cut the hit rate, pushing more checks down to the slow path.
Doesn't give true read-after-write for the 'revoke then immediately retry' case.

view.caption: An admin's grant/revoke flows into the relationship/tuple store, while the check API simply lets cached decisions in the decision cache expire by TTL.

options.name: Event-driven invalidation on change
options.description: An indexer tails the change log and proactively evicts affected decision-cache entries when tuples change, so most revokes take effect within milliseconds.
pros: The Materializer consumes the change log and proactively evicts affected cache entries, so most revokes are honored within milliseconds.
Keeps a high hit rate (no blanket short TTL) while staying close to fresh.
Reuses the same change pipeline that drives expanded-set materialization.
cons: Invalidation is asynchronous, so there's still a small race between revoke and eviction.
Fan-out of a single group change can invalidate huge swaths of cache (thundering invalidation).
No hard guarantee for the immediate retry without also using a token.

view.caption: A tuple change in the relationship/tuple store is delivered through the change log to a materializer, which proactively invalidates the affected decision-cache entries the check API reads.

steps.decisionPrompt: You revoke Bob's access, then he immediately tries the action. How do you guarantee he's denied?

concepts.definition: A version token from a write that a check can require freshness against.
concepts.whyItMatters: Lets callers demand revokes are honored, without forcing strong consistency on every check.
concepts.example: Revoke returns t_78; the next check passes min_token:t_78 to bypass stale cache.

whyNow: Revoke correctness is the subtle, must-have property of an authz system; consistency tokens are the standard mechanism and the senior signal.

recap.after: Per-call consistency: default fast, opt-in fresh after writes.
recap.newRisk: Listing 'what can I access' still requires walking the graph per object — too slow at scale.

traps.trap: Globally strong-consistent checks to be safe.
traps.why: Kills latency/throughput for the 99% of checks that don't need it.
traps.instead: Default cached; require freshness only via a token after a relevant write.

flows.name: Revoke then check: honor it with a consistency token
flows.note: A revoke returns a token; the app passes that token on the next check so it bypasses any stale cached allow (no new-enemy bug).

steps.title: 5. Listing Accessible Objects (Reverse Index)
description: Apps need 'list the docs user:42 can view' to render and filter UIs. Doing a per-object check across millions of objects is infeasible. Precompute the reverse: a materialized expanded-set index mapping subject → accessible objects per relation.

The index is derived from the relationship graph (expanding group/folder/ownership relations). 'List objects' becomes a lookup over a subject->objects index chunked by `(tenant, subject, relation, objectType, shard)`. List answers are almost always scoped — by object type, relation, tenant, folder/collection, or an upstream search query — so return results paginated with a stable page token rather than as one unbounded set.

The symmetric query — 'who can access this object O' — is NOT free from the same index; it needs its own object->subjects index (`subject_set_chunks`), and its fan-out shape differs. A doc shared with one large group is a compact tuple but millions of effective subjects, so naively expanding it is prohibitive. So list-subjects returns direct grants plus set references (group:eng#member) by default and expands a set ref into bounded, paginated user pages only on request — the caller chooses direct grants, effective subjects, or both. Like list-objects, it paginates and honors `min_token` against the shard's applied version. This powers sharing UIs, access reviews, and incident response.

Two cases the pure index doesn't cover: when the caller already has a small candidate set (the 50 docs on a page), it's cheaper to batch-filter those with checks than to read a giant index; and ABAC caveats (department, time) can't be fully materialized, so caveated tuples are post-filtered at read time against current attributes. List with a `min_token` only serves shards whose applied version is fresh enough.
Because it's derived, it can be rebuilt from the tuple store and must be kept in sync as relations change.

view.caption: The check API answers a list-accessible-objects query straight from the expanded-set index, sidestepping the policy engine's per-object walk over the relationship/tuple store.

options.name: Precomputed reverse (expanded-set) index (default)
options.description: A materializer keeps a subject-to-accessible-objects index current from the change log, so listing answers a single index read instead of per-object checks.
pros: 'Which of these 10M docs can the user see' is one index read, not 10M checks.
The Materializer keeps the index current from the change log, so reads stay cheap.
Scales to large result sets and supports server-side filtering/pagination.
cons: The index can be large and must be maintained for every subject - storage and write amplification.
It lags writes, so a just-granted object may not appear immediately.
Group changes can trigger expensive re-materialization for many subjects.

view.caption: The check API resolves a listing in one read of the precomputed expanded-set index, kept current from the relationship/tuple store, instead of evaluating each object.

options.name: Filter a candidate set with batched checks
options.description: No reverse index exists; the API takes a candidate set of objects and batches them into one policy-engine call, keeping only those the subject can access.
pros: No reverse index to build or keep fresh - always reflects current tuples.
Works well when the candidate set is already small (e.g. the 50 docs on one page).
Batches checks into one Policy Engine call to amortize overhead.
cons: Cost grows with the candidate set: filtering 10M objects is infeasible per request.
Pagination is awkward - you don't know how many candidates to fetch to fill a page.
Repeated full-corpus listing hammers the RelStore.

view.caption: With no reverse index, the check API batches a candidate set into the policy engine, which walks the relationship/tuple store, consulting the decision cache to keep only accessible objects.

steps.decisionPrompt: How do you answer 'which of these 10M documents can this user see' without 10M checks?

concepts.definition: Two precomputed indexes derived from the relation graph: subject->objects (list-objects) and object->subjects (list-subjects). The object->subjects side stores set refs, not fully expanded users, because a single grant can imply millions of subjects.
concepts.whyItMatters: Turns list/filter authorization into a lookup instead of N checks; the two directions have different fan-out, so they aren't served by one index.
concepts.example: objects: expanded_sets[user:42][view] = {doc:9, doc:13, ...}; subjects: subject_set_chunks[doc:9][view] = [user:7, group:eng#member].

whyNow: List-objects is a real product need the naive check path can't serve; the materialized reverse index is the standard answer and a clear scaling decision.

recap.newRisk: The reverse index and decision cache must update when relations/policies change.

steps.title: 6. Change Propagation and Materialization
description: When a relation or policy changes, the decision cache and expanded-set index must update or they'll serve stale answers. The ordered change log is the spine: a materializer consumes changes, updates reverse indexes, and invalidates affected cached decisions.

Invalidation must be precise but bounded: a group membership change can affect many subjects/objects (fan-out). Invalidate by the affected sets and let checks re-evaluate; the consistency token guarantees correctness for callers that need immediate effect even before materialization catches up.
Versioning every tuple by the change-log position lets caches reason about freshness uniformly.

Materializer lag is the freshness SLO of the system: track watermark lag (latest change-log version minus the version each index/cache region has applied) per tenant, namespace, and relation, and alert when it exceeds the bounded-staleness budget. Lag spikes are the early signal of an invalidation storm or a stuck consumer, and they directly bound how long a default (no-token) check can serve stale.

view.caption: A write to the relationship/tuple store flows through the change log to the materializer, which both updates the expanded-set index and invalidates stale decision-cache entries.

options.name: Eager fan-out invalidation/materialization (default)
options.description: On each change the materializer immediately recomputes the affected reverse-index shards and evicts the affected cached decisions, so reads stay correct without per-read work.
pros: Reads are cheap and correct: the index and cache are already updated when the next check arrives.
Simple mental model — derived state always reflects the latest applied change-log version.
Pairs naturally with consistency tokens: the watermark advances as fan-out completes.
cons: A widely-shared group/folder change fans out to many subjects/objects — a write spike becomes an invalidation storm.
Materializer must be partitioned and parallelized to keep fan-out latency bounded.
Briefly amplifies load right when a large change lands.

view.caption: The materializer consumes each change and eagerly updates the affected expanded-set shards and evicts the affected cached decisions.

options.name: Lazy versioned revalidation
options.description: The materializer only advances version watermarks; the index/cache aren't eagerly rewritten. A read compares its entry's version to the latest and re-evaluates on demand if stale.
pros: A huge group change is cheap to record — no immediate fan-out work.
Work is paid only for entries actually read again, not for every affected subject.
Naturally bounds the invalidation storm by deferring it.
cons: Shifts cost onto the read path: the first read after a change pays a re-evaluation.
Cold/rarely-read entries can stay logically stale for a long time (still safe via the watermark check).
Listing can be slow right after a big change as shards lazily rebuild.

view.caption: The materializer advances watermarks only; a stale read at the check API re-evaluates through the policy engine on demand rather than being pushed an update.

options.name: Hybrid priority-based materialization
options.description: Eagerly materialize hot/high-value entries (recently-read subjects, sensitive relations) and defer the long tail to lazy revalidation, prioritizing the change-log consumer queue.
pros: Keeps hot reads fast and fresh while capping the fan-out cost of giant low-traffic groups.
Lets you bound materializer load with a priority budget rather than processing fan-out blindly.
Sensitive relations can be forced eager regardless of traffic.
cons: More complex: needs hotness/priority signals and two propagation paths to operate.
Misclassifying hotness can leave an important entry on the slow lazy path.
Harder to reason about worst-case freshness across the two paths.

view.caption: The materializer splits the change stream by priority — eagerly rebuilding hot shards and evicting hot cache entries, while deferring the long tail to lazy revalidation.

steps.decisionPrompt: Removing a user from a group should revoke access to thousands of documents. How does that propagate?
whyNow: Change propagation is what keeps derived state correct; tying it to the change log + tokens closes the consistency story.

concepts.definition: Driving derived indexes and cache invalidation from an ordered change stream.
concepts.whyItMatters: Keeps caches/indexes consistent with the tuple store and bounds propagation.
concepts.example: Group membership change -> invalidate affected expanded sets + cached decisions.

recap.before: Derived state could go stale on changes.
recap.after: Changes propagate via the log; tokens cover the gap.
recap.newRisk: Hot objects/groups cause invalidation fan-out; availability of the central service is critical.

failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Affected sets/decisions invalidate; immediate callers using a fresh token get correct results before full materialization.
failureDrills.mitigation: Change-log fan-out invalidation + consistency tokens as the correctness backstop.

flows.name: Change → materialize index + invalidate caches
flows.note: A tuple/policy change flows through the ordered change log; the materializer updates reverse indexes and invalidates affected cached decisions.

steps.title: 7. Availability, Auditing, and Multi-Region
description: Authz is on every request, so its outage cascades to every app. Run it highly available: stateless check tiers, replicated tuple/policy stores, and SDK-side caches so brief authz blips don't hard-fail apps (with a safe deny-by-default fallback for sensitive actions).

Multi-region needs an explicit ordering contract, not just 'replicated regionally'. Writes for a tenant are pinned to that tenant's home region/shard, which owns a per-tenant ordered change log (a global total order isn't needed — checks only compare versions within a tenant); the home log replicates to other regions, and consistency tokens are positions in that log. Each region's caches/indexes carry their own applied-version watermark. A `min_token` check that lands in a lagging region (watermark < token) does NOT serve the local stale entry: it waits a bounded budget for the watermark to catch up, else routes the check to the home region, else returns a retryable consistency error — and for sensitive actions it denies/steps-up rather than waiting. Default minimize_latency checks read the local replica freely.

SDK-cache fallback during an authz outage is sensitivity-classed, not blanket. A cache miss always fails closed (deny). A cached allow is only honored for low-risk reads, with a short TTL and a watermark/expiration bound; for admin, delete, payment, or data-export actions a stale cached allow is NOT honored — those fail closed (deny or step-up) even if a recent allow is cached.

Treat the service as a control plane too: a policy/schema write is validated (relations resolve, recursion/depth budget, cycles handled, migration-compatible), published as shadow where it is evaluated alongside the active version so allow/deny deltas are measured before promotion to active, and can be rolled back — decisions cached under a rolled-back version are invalidated by policy version just like a tuple change. Tenant IDs partition tuples, policies, cache keys, and list indexes for isolation.

Every decision and grant/revoke is auditable. The change log doubles as the grant/revoke record (actor, reason, old/new tuple), and each check can emit a decision record (decisionId, result, reason code, policy/tuple version, caller) — but at 1M checks/s audit ingestion is async and tiered: full logs for denies/admin/sensitive actions, sampled or aggregated routine allows, hot searchable storage aging to cold object storage, and privacy redaction of identifiers — enough to explain any allow/deny for compliance without the audit path blocking or dominating cost.

Operate it on explicit signals: per-tenant/namespace/relation materializer watermark lag (the freshness SLO), cache hit rate and slow-path QPS, hot-key concentration, per-tenant quotas, and explain/debug rate limits.

view.caption: The application's check travels through the authz client SDK to the check API, which serves verdicts from the decision cache and, on a miss, evaluates via the policy engine over the policy/schema store.

options.name: Fail-closed with cached fallback (default)
options.description: When the authz service is unreachable the in-process SDK cache serves recent decisions, and any uncached request is denied so an outage never silently grants access.
pros: When the authz service is unreachable, the SDK serves recent cached decisions, so most reads keep working safely.
On a true miss it denies (fail-closed), so an outage can never silently grant access.
Local decision cache means a regional outage degrades gracefully rather than hard-failing every app.
cons: Actions whose decisions aren't cached get denied during the outage - availability cost for safety.
Stale cached grants persist until TTL, so a revoke made during the outage may not apply yet.
Each app must embed and trust the SDK cache, increasing client footprint.

view.caption: The authz client SDK serves recent decisions from the decision cache when the check API is unreachable, denying any uncached request loaded from the policy/schema store.

options.name: Fail-open (allow on outage)
options.description: When the authz service is down the SDK allows the action by default, so dependent apps keep serving rather than blocking on the check.
pros: Maximizes availability: dependent apps keep serving even when authz is fully down.
Simple to reason about - no partial-denial weirdness during an incident.
Appropriate for low-stakes, read-mostly surfaces where a brief over-grant is tolerable.
cons: An authz outage becomes a security incident: everyone is allowed everything.
Unacceptable for sensitive actions (admin, payments, deletes).
Hard to bound the blast radius of what got wrongly allowed.

view.caption: When the check API is down the authz client SDK allows the action by default, bypassing the policy/schema store rather than blocking the calling application.

options.name: Multi-region replicated check path
options.description: Each region runs its own check API and cache fed by the replicated per-tenant change log, so default checks stay in-region; a min_token check in a lagging region waits, routes to the tenant's home region, or fails retryably, and a regional outage reroutes traffic.
pros: Each region runs its own Check API + cache fed by the tenant's replicated change log, so a regional outage just sheds that region.
No fail-open/closed tradeoff for whole-region loss - traffic reroutes to a healthy region.
Lower latency too: default checks stay in-region; only min_token reads that outrun the local watermark route home.
cons: Cross-region replication of changes adds lag, so a min_token revoke may wait for or route to the home region.
More infrastructure and cost to run the data plane everywhere.
Writes pin to a tenant home region, so a home-region outage blocks new grants for that tenant until failover.

view.caption: A region-local check API serves verdicts from its decision cache, kept fresh by a materializer that consumes the global change log so requests stay in-region.

steps.decisionPrompt: The authz service has a regional outage. What should happen to dependent apps?
whyNow: Closing on availability/audit/multi-region addresses the 'don't take down every app' requirement and the compliance angle.

concepts.definition: A per-decision record (id, result, reason code, policy/tuple version, caller) plus the change log as a grant/revoke trail.
concepts.whyItMatters: A shared authz service must prove who could do what and explain any allow/deny for compliance and incident review.
concepts.example: decision d_5f2: deny, reason no_path, policyVersion 14, caller svc:docs.

concepts.definition: Schema/policy versions are validated, staged draft→shadow→active with a rollback path, and shadow-evaluated (old vs candidate) so allow/deny deltas are measured before promotion. Decisions cached under a rolled-back version are invalidated by version.
concepts.whyItMatters: A bad relation rewrite can over-grant or deny across many apps; shadow comparison and version-keyed invalidation make rollout and reversal safe without re-granting.
concepts.example: Publish rewrite as shadow, measure that it would flip 0.2% of denies to allow, then promote to active or roll back to version 14.

concepts.definition: A consistency token is a position in a tenant's home-region change log replicated to other regions; each region's caches carry an applied-version watermark, and a min_token read below the local watermark waits, routes to the home region, or fails retryably.
concepts.whyItMatters: 'Replicated regionally' does not by itself make revokes globally safe — the watermark-vs-token check and home-region routing are what prevent a lagging region from serving a stale allow.
concepts.example: Revoke at home returns t_78; a check in a lagging EU region whose watermark is t_70 waits or routes home rather than serving its pre-revoke cache.

recap.after: HA, regionally-replicated, auditable authz with safe fallbacks.
recap.newRisk: Cross-region staleness and fallback policy — bounded by tokens and conservative deny-by-default.

bottlenecks.issue: Central check service is a global SPOF/bottleneck.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Stateless scale-out + SDK caches + regional replicas; deny-by-default on hard failure.

failureDrills.scenario: A min_token check lands in a region whose change-log replica lags behind the token.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The region refuses to serve its stale cache: it waits a bounded budget for the watermark to reach the token, else routes the check to the tenant's home region; if neither completes in budget it returns a retryable consistency error (deny/step-up for sensitive actions). It never serves a pre-revoke allow.
failureDrills.mitigation: Per-region applied-version watermark compared against the token + bounded wait + home-region routing + retryable error.

flows.name: min_token check in a lagging region: wait, route home, or fail
flows.note: A sensitive check carries a token newer than the local region's watermark. The region does not serve its stale cache — it waits briefly, then routes to the tenant's home region (or returns a retryable error).

messages.label: watermark catches up within wait budget

else.label: still lagging — route to home region

messages.label: decision (or retryable error if over budget)

finalDesign.title: Final Design — Centralized ReBAC Authorization
finalDesign.description: Apps call a stateless check API (via an SDK with in-process decision caching) asking 'can S do A on O?'. The policy engine evaluates versioned relationship tuples (ReBAC) plus RBAC roles / ABAC caveats as a bounded, cached, short-circuiting graph walk. Writes to the tuple store are idempotent and conditional and flow through an ordered change log that both returns consistency tokens — so callers can demand revokes are honored (no new-enemy bug) while default checks stay fast — and serves as the grant/revoke audit trail. Caches, index shards, and regions carry applied-version watermarks so a min_token check never serves a stale allow. A materializer maintains chunked expanded-set reverse indexes for 'list accessible objects' and invalidates affected cached decisions on change. Schema/policy versions roll out (draft→shadow→active) and roll back; every decision can be logged for audit. The service is stateless, regionally replicated, tenant-isolated, and deny-by-default on failure.

view.caption: The application checks via the SDK and check API, which serves from the decision cache or evaluates through the policy engine over the relationship/tuple and policy/schema stores, lists from the expanded-set index, and propagates admin writes via the change log and materializer.

functional.how: Check API + policy engine over the relationship graph.
Tuple writes via the change log with tokens.
Two materialized reverse indexes: subject->objects for list-objects, and object->subjects (set refs, not full expansion) for list-subjects; both paginated and min_token-gated.

functional.requirement: Centralized policy used by many applications
functional.how: One stateless check API + policy engine over shared tuple/policy/schema stores; apps embed only a thin caching SDK, never policy logic.
Versioned policy/schema (draft→shadow→active→rolled_back), change-log + decision audit records, and tenant IDs threaded through tuples, caches, and indexes.

nonFunctional.how: Decision cache + SDK cache + bounded graph walk.
Stateless check tier scaled horizontally; SDK + decision caches absorb ~99% of load so only ~1% reaches the policy engine/tuple store.
Policy model with default-deny and safe fallbacks.

say: Confirm a centralized check on every request: sub-10ms, deny-by-default.
Pin correctness (revokes honored) and high availability as hard goals.
Establish the need for both checks and 'list what I can access'.
Choose the model: relationship tuples (ReBAC) + RBAC/ABAC on top.
Check = bounded, cached graph walk via a stateless check API.
Consistency tokens to honor revokes safely (the new-enemy problem).
Tradeoffs: caching staleness vs latency, invalidation fan-out, model expressiveness.
Mention policy testing and delegation/inheritance as extensions.

expectations: Uses RBAC and a check endpoint.
Designs ReBAC tuples + bounded cached graph walk.
Materializes a reverse index for listing.
Reasons about the new-enemy problem and invalidation fan-out.
Designs change-log materialization and multi-region freshness.
Discusses model tradeoffs (RBAC/ABAC/ReBAC) and HA/audit precisely.

followUps: How do you prevent the new-enemy problem (stale allow after revoke) precisely?
How would you test and safely roll out a policy change?
How do you bound invalidation fan-out when a huge group changes?
How would you support delegated administration and permission inheritance?
When is RBAC sufficient and ReBAC overkill?

links.groupDescription: Real-world authorization platforms and production architecture case studies.

links.groupDescription: Foundational access-control models and security design guidance.
